label: Group Theory
definition: The mathematical study of symmetry, formalized as a set of transformations that can be composed and undone, providing the language for invariance and equivariance in machine learning.

body.text: Group theory is the mathematics of symmetry. A group is a set of elements together with a way to combine any two of them that is associative, has an identity element that does nothing, and gives every element an inverse that undoes it. The canonical examples are collections of transformations: the rotations of a square, the permutations of a deck of cards, the translations of the plane, the invertible matrices of a given size. In each case the group law is composition (do one transformation, then another), the identity is leaving things alone, and the inverse is reversing a transformation. A group is therefore a precise account of what it means for a system to have symmetry: the transformations that leave some essential structure unchanged form a group.

The single most useful idea is the group action, the formal statement that a group operates on a space by transforming its points while respecting composition. A group acting on a set partitions it into orbits (the points reachable from one another by some group element) and assigns each point a stabilizer (the elements that fix it), and the relationship between orbits, stabilizers, and the group's size is one of the first powerful counting results in the subject. Representation theory pushes this further by realizing abstract group elements as concrete matrices acting on a vector-space, so that the symmetry can be studied with the tools of linear algebra. Decomposing a representation into its irreducible pieces is the algebraic heart of the fourier-transform and of spectral-decomposition on symmetric domains.

Group theory matters in machine learning because the right inductive bias is very often a symmetry. If a task is unchanged when its input is transformed by some group, the model should be too, and encoding that directly is far more efficient than hoping the network learns it from data. The two precise versions of this are invariance (the output does not change when the input is transformed) and equivariance (the output transforms in lockstep with the input). The convolutional-neural-network is the famous case: convolution is exactly the operation that is equivariant to the translation group, which is why a feature detector that fires on an object in one corner of an image fires on the same object anywhere. Pooling then converts that translation equivariance into approximate translation invariance.

Geometric deep learning generalizes this insight by treating the choice of architecture as the choice of a symmetry group. If the data lives on a sphere, a graph, or a set, you ask which group of transformations should leave the answer unchanged (rotations, node permutations, set permutations) and build layers that are equivariant to that group by construction. Group-equivariant networks for the rotation group give vision models that recognize objects regardless of orientation; permutation-equivariant networks underpin architectures for sets and graphs; the self-attention mechanism is permutation-equivariant over its tokens, which is precisely why positional information has to be added back in separately. In every case, group theory tells you exactly what structure to bake in and guarantees the network honors it.

When the symmetries form a continuum rather than a discrete list, as with all rotations or all translations, the relevant object is a lie-group, which fuses group structure with the smooth geometry of differential-geometry. The discrete and continuous cases share the same philosophy: identify the transformations under which the problem is invariant, recognize that they form a group, and use that group to constrain the model. This is why group theory, abstract as it looks, has become one of the most practically consequential pieces of pure mathematics for modern deep learning, supplying the rigorous backbone for equivariance, weight sharing, and the design of architectures that match the symmetry of their data.
